original_text: The evidence for the clinical indications found in this guideline includes 6 published peer reviewed articles, 5 specialty society or other evidence-based guidelines, and 2 book sections.
normalized_text: The evidence for the clinical indications found in this guideline includes 6 published peer reviewed articles, 5 specialty society or other evidence-based guidelines, and 2 book sections.

original_text: Analysis of national hospital discharge data for a commercially insured population shows 70.2% of patients seen in an emergency department with a principal diagnosis of heart failure were admitted to inpatient care. ( 5 ) (EG 3) Analysis of national hospital discharge data for a Medicare-insured population shows 81.5% of patients seen in an emergency department with a principal diagnosis of heart failure were admitted to inpatient care. ( 5 ) (EG 3) Analysis of an all-payer database shows a mean length of stay of 5.4 days for patients admitted to inpatient care. ( 6 ) (EG 3)
normalized_text: Analysis of national hospital discharge data for a commercially insured population shows 70.2% of patients seen in an emergency department with a principal diagnosis of heart failure were admitted to inpatient care. ( 5 ) (EG 3) Analysis of national hospital discharge data for a Medicare-insured population shows 81.5% of patients seen in an emergency department with a principal diagnosis of heart failure were admitted to inpatient care. ( 5 ) (EG 3) Analysis of an all-payer database shows a mean length of stay of 5.4 days for patients admitted to inpatient care. ( 6 ) (EG 3)

original_text: An evidence-based specialty society guideline notes that patients with decompensated heart failure (eg, worsening pulmonary edema with dyspnea, tachypnea, and/or hypoxemia) may require hospitalization with rapid initiation and escalation (if necessary) of IV diuretics in addition to other medications recommended to reduce morbidity and mortality in heart failure patients. ( 1 ) (EG 2) A specialty society guideline, an emergency medicine textbook, and a narrative review state that patients with severe presentations (eg, dangerous arrhythmia, myocardial ischemia, hypotension, severe or persistent altered mental status, acute kidney injury) may require evaluation for intensive care. ( 1 ) ( 7 ) (EG 2) An emergency medicine textbook recommends inpatient care for heart failure patients who present with findings categorizing them as at moderate or high risk of short-term mortality and morbidity. ( 3 ) (EG 2) Examples of these findings include hypotension, need for respiratory assistance (ie, CPAP, BPAP, mechanical ventilation), dangerous arrhythmia, myocardial ischemia, persistent pulmonary edema leading to hypoxemia that persists despite an adequate trial of IV diuretic therapy, new or worsening hyponatremia, severe edema, and acute kidney injury. ( 3 ) (EG 2) A narrative review recommends hospitalization for patients with cardiogenic shock, severe hypoxia, hypotension, myocardial ischemia, uncontrolled arrhythmia, severe electrolyte abnormalities, or acute kidney injury, as well as for patients with severe congestion who have an inadequate response to initial therapy. ( 7 ) (EG 2)
normalized_text: An evidence-based specialty society guideline notes that patients with decompensated heart failure (eg, worsening pulmonary edema with dyspnea, tachypnea, and/or hypoxemia) may require hospitalization with rapid initiation and escalation (if necessary) of IV diuretics in addition to other medications recommended to reduce morbidity and mortality in heart failure patients. ( 1 ) (EG 2) A specialty society guideline, an emergency medicine textbook, and a narrative review state that patients with severe presentations (eg, dangerous arrhythmia, myocardial ischemia, hypotension, severe or persistent altered mental status, acute kidney injury) may require evaluation for intensive care. ( 1 ) ( 7 ) (EG 2) An emergency medicine textbook recommends inpatient care for heart failure patients who present with findings categorizing them as at moderate or high risk of short-term mortality and morbidity. ( 3 ) (EG 2) Examples of these findings include hypotension, need for respiratory assistance (ie, CPAP, BPAP, mechanical ventilation), dangerous arrhythmia, myocardial ischemia, persistent pulmonary edema leading to hypoxemia that persists despite an adequate trial of IV diuretic therapy, new or worsening hyponatremia, severe edema, and acute kidney injury. ( 3 ) (EG 2) A narrative review recommends hospitalization for patients with cardiogenic shock, severe hypoxia, hypotension, myocardial ischemia, uncontrolled arrhythmia, severe electrolyte abnormalities, or acute kidney injury, as well as for patients with severe congestion who have an inadequate response to initial therapy. ( 7 ) (EG 2)

original_text: A specialty society guideline recommends 4 medication classes as guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) for adult patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF): beta-blockers, renin-angiotensin inhibitors (angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor preferred), aldosterone blockers, and sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors. ( 1 ) (EG 2) This same guideline recommends the following for patients hospitalized with HFrEF absent clear contraindications: pre-existing GDMT should be continued and optimized (titration to therapeutic dose begun as inpatient), GDMT should not be discontinued due to mild decrease in renal function or blood pressure, GDMT should be initiated during hospitalization when clinical stability has been achieved (if all drug classes not on board), and, if GDMT is discontinued, it should be reinstated as soon as possible. ( 1 ) (EG 2) For heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), an expert panel recommends sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors, aldosterone blockers, and either an angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor (preferred) or an angiotensin receptor blocker as GDMT, with the addition of beta-blockers reserved for specific indications, including comorbid atrial fibrillation, myocardial infarction within 3 years, or angina. ( 22 ) (EG 2)
normalized_text: A specialty society guideline recommends 4 medication classes as guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) for adult patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF): beta-blockers, renin-angiotensin inhibitors (angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor preferred), aldosterone blockers, and sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors. ( 1 ) (EG 2) This same guideline recommends the following for patients hospitalized with HFrEF absent clear contraindications: pre-existing GDMT should be continued and optimized (titration to therapeutic dose begun as inpatient), GDMT should not be discontinued due to mild decrease in renal function or blood pressure, GDMT should be initiated during hospitalization when clinical stability has been achieved (if all drug classes not on board), and, if GDMT is discontinued, it should be reinstated as soon as possible. ( 1 ) (EG 2) For heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), an expert panel recommends sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors, aldosterone blockers, and either an angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor (preferred) or an angiotensin receptor blocker as GDMT, with the addition of beta-blockers reserved for specific indications, including comorbid atrial fibrillation, myocardial infarction within 3 years, or angina. ( 22 ) (EG 2)

original_text: For children with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, a specialty society guideline recommends routine use of renin-angiotensin inhibitors, with the addition of diuretics for patients with fluid retention and ventricular dysfunction; other drugs that are recommended as reasonable additional therapies include beta-blockers, digoxin, and ivabradine. ( 4 ) (EG 2) For children who have heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, the same guideline recommends diuretics to achieve euvolemia and recommends considering a sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitor for symptoms. ( 4 ) (EG 2)
normalized_text: For children with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, a specialty society guideline recommends routine use of renin-angiotensin inhibitors, with the addition of diuretics for patients with fluid retention and ventricular dysfunction; other drugs that are recommended as reasonable additional therapies include beta-blockers, digoxin, and ivabradine. ( 4 ) (EG 2) For children who have heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, the same guideline recommends diuretics to achieve euvolemia and recommends considering a sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitor for symptoms. ( 4 ) (EG 2)

original_text: A specialty society clinical guideline, based upon a systematic review of the evidence, concludes that heart failure patients with evidence of significant fluid overload should be promptly treated with IV loop diuretics (eg, initial dose twice the home regimen in terms of oral to parenteral milligram equivalents) and titrated to resolution of clinical signs and symptoms of congestion (eg, serial doubling of dosage as bolus or continuous infusion). ( 1 ) (EG 2) This guideline further recommends increasing the diuretic dose or adding a second diuretic (eg, thiazide diuretic or aldosterone antagonist with diuretic properties) for patients with inadequate diuresis until an effective regimen is identified that is adequate to relieve congestion. ( 1 ) (EG 2) A pediatric guideline states that diuretics are first-line therapy for children with heart failure and fluid overload. ( 4 ) (EG 2)

( 1 ) (EG 2) This guideline further recommends increasing the diuretic dose or adding a second diuretic (eg, thiazide diuretic or aldosterone antagonist with diuretic properties) for patients with inadequate diuresis until an effective regimen is identified that is adequate to relieve congestion. ( 1 ) (EG 2) A pediatric guideline states that diuretics are first-line therapy for children with heart failure and fluid overload. ( 4 ) (EG 2)

original_text: Analysis of 525,037 patients (median age 70 years) admitted with decompensated heart failure to an advanced therapy center (ATC) (defined as a hospital performing at least one heart transplant or left ventricular assist device per year) from the Nationwide Readmissions Database reported 25% of patients were discharged in 2.2 days or less. ( 35 ) (EG 2) In the same study, analysis of 2,331,690 patients (median age 74 years) admitted with decompensated heart failure to a non-ATC hospital reported 25% of patients were discharged in 1.9 days or less. ( 35 ) (EG 2) Analysis of 950,045 adult patients admitted with heart failure from the Nationwide Inpatient Sample database found that 25% of patients were discharged in 2.1 days or less. ( 34 ) (EG 2) Analysis of national hospital discharge data shows 24% of hospitalized patients with the primary diagnosis of heart failure were discharged in 2 days or less. ( 6 ) (EG 3)

original_text: Use of this MCG care guideline helps the clinician identify patient-specific complex clinical factors that make it reasonable to expect a necessity for hospital care across 2 or more midnights. The evidence-based clinical criteria assist the clinician in the decision to appropriately admit a patient to inpatient care. For Medicare enrollees, MCG care guidelines also support the clinician in the decision to appropriately admit a patient to inpatient care when there is not an expectation of 2 or more midnights of hospital care (eg, CMS' Two-Midnight Rule exceptions).
normalized_text: Use of this MCG care guideline helps the clinician identify patient-specific complex clinical factors that make it reasonable to expect a necessity for hospital care across 2 or more midnights. The evidence-based clinical criteria assist the clinician in the decision to appropriately admit a patient to inpatient care. For Medicare enrollees, MCG care guidelines also support the clinician in the decision to appropriately admit a patient to inpatient care when there is not an expectation of 2 or more midnights of hospital care (eg, CMS' Two-Midnight Rule exceptions).

original_text: Use of these evidence-based clinical criteria to support decision making around the need for inpatient treatment is of clinical benefit to the patient and is crucial for quality patient care. Use of evidence-based clinical criteria ensures patients receive the most appropriate care for their specific condition, reduces unnecessary risks, promotes recovery, and provides a standard that can reduce disparities in care. Evidence-based clinical criteria not only help align treatment decisions with best practice, but they can also help reduce unwarranted variation in care by fostering consistency and equality in healthcare provision across regions and facilities. Appropriate use of the evidence-based clinical criteria in conjunction with the Supplemental Medicare Criteria ensures Medicare patients receive full access to Medicare benefits (eg, inpatient care), without risk of delay or decreased access, based on variables such as clinical severity of illness, length of provision of medically necessary hospital-level care, or satisfaction of specified Medicare criteria as directed by CMS. Use of these criteria will help ensure that the patient receives necessary care in the appropriate setting.

Use of these criteria will help ensure that the patient receives necessary care in the appropriate setting.

original_text: This guideline supplements but does not replace, modify, or supersede existing Medicare regulations or applicable National Coverage Determinations (NCDs) or Local Coverage Determinations (LCDs).
